chunk_summary: Outpatient CMAM programs in India define recovery criteria based on MUAC or WHZ thresholds, oedema-free periods, and maximum treatment durations, with structured referrals for non-responders.
learning_objectives: Understand the anthropometric recovery criteria for outpatient SAM management in India.
Learn the importance of consistent recovery standards and structured referral protocols for non-responders.
Identify triggers for early referral, including weight gain thresholds and persistent symptoms.
Explore the role of MUAC and WHZ in monitoring recovery trends.
key_facts: Recovery criteria: MUAC ≥125 mm or WHZ ≥-2, both oedema-free for ≥2 weeks.
Maximum outpatient treatment duration: 12–16 weeks in Indian protocols.
Non-response triggers: weight gain <5 g/kg/day, persistent oedema, appetite-test failure, or recurrent infections.
Structured referrals aim to address silent failures and underlying conditions like TB/HIV or malabsorption.
Percentage weight gain is not recommended as a sole cure criterion due to bias from admission weight.
reference_quote: A widely used WHO criterion for discharge from nutritional treatment is: MUAC ≥125 mm with no bilateral pitting oedema for at least 2 weeks, or WHZ ≥-2 with no bilateral pitting oedema for at least 2 weeks. (Source: Reference 2)
document_key_topics: Outpatient recovery criteria for SAM management
Maximum treatment duration and referral protocols
